title: Capstone: Todo List
description: Build a todo list with immutable updates — add, toggle, filter, and tally.
objectives: Update an array of objects without mutating it (return new arrays)
Use spread syntax to copy an object with one field changed
Aggregate a collection into a single result with `reduce`

tests.name: addTodo: empty list gets id 1
tests.call: addTodo([], 'first').map(t => ({id: t.id, done: t.done, text: t.text}))

tests.name: addTodo: ids increment
tests.call: (() => { let t = []; t = addTodo(t, 'a'); t = addTodo(t, 'b'); t = addTodo(t, 'c'); return t.map(x => x.id); })()

tests.name: toggleTodo: toggles back when called twice
toggleTodo: missing id is a no-op

recap: Real apps don't usually overwrite shared state — they make a NEW version and replace the old one. That's what `[...arr, x]` and `{ ...obj, key: value }` do: they're not magic, they're just shorthand for 'copy this and tweak one thing.' You also met `reduce`, which collapses a list to a single value (a count, a sum, an object). Pair filter/map/reduce with immutable updates and you have the entire core of modern JS state management.
practicePrompts: Add a `clearCompleted(todos)` that returns the list with all done items removed.
Add an `oldestActive(todos)` that returns the active todo with the smallest id (the one created first that's still not done).

practiceExercises.title: Rename a todo
practiceExercises.prompt: Write `renameTodo(todos, id, newText)` that returns a NEW array. The matching todo gets a new `text` field; everything else (id, done) stays the same. Other todos are untouched.
practiceExercises.goal: Practice a single-field object update with spread syntax.
hints: Use map. For each todo: matching id → new object with new text; otherwise → return the todo unchanged.
Spread to keep the other fields: `{ ...t, text: newText }`
`return todos.map(t => t.id === id ? { ...t, text: newText } : t);`

practiceExercises.title: Longest todo (with reduce)
practiceExercises.prompt:
Write `longestText(todos)` that returns the `text` of whichever todo has the longest text. If two are tied, return the one that comes first in the list. For an empty list, return `""`.

Write it with `reduce` — that's the point of this exercise. You'll start with an empty-string accumulator and update it whenever you find a longer text.
practiceExercises.goal: Practice `reduce` for picking the best of a list — the canonical 'fold a list to one value' shape.
hints: `reduce` walks the list and keeps a running answer. Start with `""`. On each todo, decide: is its text longer than what I have so far?
Inside the callback: `return t.text.length > longest.length ? t.text : longest;` — keep the new one only if it's strictly longer.
`return todos.reduce((longest, t) => t.text.length > longest.length ? t.text : longest, "");`

practiceExercises.title: Completion fraction
practiceExercises.prompt: Write `completedFraction(todos)` that returns a number between 0 and 1 representing the share of done todos. For an empty list, return 0 (don't divide by zero).
practiceExercises.goal: Compose a count with a guard against the empty case.
hints: First check: if `todos.length === 0`, return 0. That handles the divide-by-zero case.
Otherwise count the done ones with filter, then divide by total.
